data.title: Serve Robotics: A Hold Until We See Lower Risk And Improved Financials
data.description:
Introduction

Serve Robotics (SERV) is a robotics company that began life in 2017 as the internal robotics division of Postmates, and was spun out on its own following the acquisition of Postmates by Uber in 2020. The company is focused on last-mile delivery in food service and convenience. Some of the tailwinds that the firm highlights for its robotics adoption include general advancements in AI, which make the hardware it needs relatively cheaper, and the overall improved safety that could occur once fewer humans are on the roads driving and making deliveries. Currently, SERV is a hold - the idea is promising, but will require some strategic execution to become reality and will need improvement within its financials. In the following section, we will review some highlights and news from the company that are relevant to its growth.

Earnings Review

The firm reported earnings this week - delivery volumes grew 66% quarter over quarter, which represented a 300% increase from the same quarter in 2024. They were also able to successfully launch in Chicago and enter their new strategic relationship with DoorDash to increase their delivery partnerships.

SERV reported third-quarter revenues of $687K, which was 209% higher than the third quarter of 2024. They have also forecasted a 10x increase in revenue for 2026 based on their preliminary projections, which should be taken with a conservative approach in case they do not meet these lofty expectations. The firm does have $210M in liquidity now, which was also boosted by a $100M share offering they completed in October.

Overall, this was a solid earnings report for a growing company with lots to prove. We are looking forward to increased execution excellence and, hopefully, increased partnerships as SERV continues to prove its concept and product-market fit.

Company News and Highlights

The overall TAM is significant, and the firm’s share is growing

According to the SERV investor presentation from August 2025, the overall addressable market for the firm is a whopping $450B by 2030. Please see the image below from the deck.

While this is a massive forecast, the firm has been able to steadily grow its share and prove that its operations are well run. See the below chart of growth in the past few years since 2022.

The average growth rate of the firm’s deliveries is very strong, and the high reliability factor also leads us to believe that at this point, the need and value of SERV seems to exist. The graph is growing quickly, and presuming that the firm can continue to maintain the reliability while growing rapidly, SERV could be well positioned to take more and more of the overall TAM.

Keystone's partnership with UberEats has proven the concept works and given the firm plenty of data to iterate on.

A major pipeline for SERV is their deal with UberEats for 2,000 robots through the end of 2025. These robots will serve 4 major markets for the company: Los Angeles, Miami, Dallas, and Atlanta. Further expansion includes Chicago as the next big city, with additional metros in the pipeline. This partnership has allowed the firm to test and iterate on their product, and has been helpful in giving SERV additional data to optimize its systems and machine-learning models. As a result, SERV is projecting that the latest generation of their robots will be 65% cheaper to run and operate compared to their second generation, and it will have faster response times and be built to last longer. See the below image for specifications of this latest model.

We believe that the firm has taken the right approach with this development, as they are able to take the learnings from their existing partnership and hone in on weaknesses to better design their robots for the future. We are excited to see what the execution of these further generations will be and are hopeful that management will be able to stick with their plan and vision.

Overall, consumers are in a tough spot right now, which is hurting delivery businesses

Broadly speaking, the overall economy right now is looking poor for consumers. There have been steady layoffs throughout different markets, the ongoing government shutdown is already the longest in history, and from the latest earnings of UberEats competitor DoorDash, it would appear that consumers are tightening up budgets to trim frivolous delivery spending. These signs are not good for the overall economy and certainly not great for delivery services that are considered a luxury expense. This downturn should be temporary, and SERV is a long-term investment if you believe in the robotics trend of the future, but we believe that this economic situation at the moment could lead to better buying opportunities down the line. It is absolutely something to stay aware of to determine whether SERV can keep innovating despite these macro headwinds.

Financial Analysis

We look at the overall trends for SERV to understand how their financials have changed in recent years.

Let’s start with revenue:

Note: Since we only have 4 full years of data and one of them has $0 in revenue, we also included a datapoint for the trailing twelve months (TTM). We color it differently to highlight that it is not a full year's worth of results and has some overlap with the prior year (2024).

Overall, we see positive growth, with a huge increase in 2024 to $1.8M in revenue compared to $0.2M in the previous year, representing a 9x increase. However, when looking at the TTM numbers, we see that there is a decline. This could mean that the company is facing a decline in revenue.

Digging deeper, we see that the difference is primarily associated with the quarter ending March 31st. In 2024, the company posted revenues of $0.9M for that quarter, while in 2025 it was only $0.4M. In the March 2024 quarterly report, the company mentioned that $0.85M of the $0.9M in revenue came from one customer: Magna.

Looking into the most recent quarterly report that covered the quarter ending June 2025, the company reported that out of their revenues of $0.6M, Magna accounted for 39% of it and Uber accounted for 31%, for a total of 70% of revenue coming from just 2 customers. According to the March 2025 quarterly report (the quarter where we saw the big quarter-over-quarter revenue drop), Magna and Uber combined made up 83% of revenues.

Based on these findings, we see that the company’s success is very much reliant on only 2 customers. Depending on the quarter, these 2 customers make up anywhere from 70% to 94% of total revenue, meaning that any changes in revenue trends can mostly be attributed to these 2 customers. This is a very risky position to be in. Even a small decrease in business from 1 customer will have meaningful impacts on SERV’s profits. In their March 2025 quarterly report, the company seems to agree, as they said that the quarter-over-quarter decline was “due to a decrease in software services revenue, which may be inconsistent in the future”.

Looking at net income now:

We see that net income continues to be more and more negative. We see that the negative net income is driven by:

  • A negative gross profit to start with. In the TTM, SERV had a gross profit of -$5.1M compared to a net income of -$55.2M, so this is not the largest contributor to losses, but it certainly does not help

  • Operating expenses, primarily R&D and SG&A. The values of these 2 expenses change over time, but roughly speaking, they have been about the same for the most recent quarters, contributing about equally to the net loss.

Overall, we are not surprised to see the company have a net loss that is growing and see that it is primarily driven by operating expenses. This is normal for a company of this size and age. However, we have some concerns with the negative gross profits. If the product the company is selling costs more for them to make/operate than the revenues they are getting, SERV is not in a very good place. We hope that the company is able to find a way to reduce their cost of revenue or increase their prices to ensure the base product is profitable.

Risks

SERV is acquired by a larger player

A risk to the thesis of hold would be that SERV is acquired by a larger player as the robotics race heats up and more firms want a piece of the pie. At only ~$760M in market cap, it would be a relatively small target for a larger firm that wants exposure to the robotics industry. Further, as M&A activity has grown in this less regulated environment under the Trump administration, it is always possible that smaller, niche players like SERV become takeover targets.

Customer Concentration and Competition

As we discussed, 2 customers make up 70% to 94% of SERV’s revenue, which is a risky place to be in as any hiccup with either customer could significantly impact SERV’s financials. One additional risk that comes out of this is competition, especially in regard to Uber. We see that for their ride sharing business, Uber has partnered with Waymo and Nvidia to bring self-driving cars to their fleet. On their autonomous page, Uber lists 18 partners they are working with (including SERV). Given that 1) there is a lot of competition in the autonomous market, 2) that Uber is actively partnered with many companies in this market in addition to SERV and 3) that the problem of autonomous delivery bots is arguably simpler and less risky than autonomous vehicles, we think that there is a very real possibility that SERV will need to fight hard to keep their Uber business.

Conclusion

SERV has an innovative product and is working on addressing a fairly large TAM. They have been able to iterate following the insights gleaned from their UberEats partnership, but are currently operating in an environment where consumers are pinching their wallets more and avoiding higher expense delivery costs. In addition, the company faces some financial headwinds, including inconsistent revenue, negative gross margins, and extremely high customer concentrations. Coupled with the risk of competition, we see a lot of potential red flags for the company.

However, we do see some positive points, such as their growth in delivery volume and a so far seemingly good partnership with Uber. Overall, we rate this as a hold. While the company has good growth prospects, we hope to see better margins, expansion in customer base, and a proven competitive moat before we consider a buy on the company.

data.title: HDV: A Defensive Dividend Play
data.description:
With a quality bias, the iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) is a low-beta dividend fund that may be appealing for investors looking for capital preservation and income as interest rates drop. This article breaks down HDV’s sector exposure and holdings, comparing its valuation, performance volatility, and tradability with the benchmark and other dividend-oriented funds.

ETF Description & Highlights

HDV tracks the Morningstar Dividend Yield Focus Index, which is composed of companies of all sizes that exhibit financial strength and high dividends compared to the broader market.

The index employs a dual screening methodology, including quality and financial health. First, companies are assigned an economic moat rating defined as wide, narrow, or none, according to Morningstar’s methodology, taking into consideration prospects for their return of capital and competitive advantages. In parallel, a second screen is applied to measure companies’ probability of default, with only the top 50% of them within their sector, and also rated as wide or narrow moat, being selected for the final step. From this pool, 75 companies with the highest dividend yield are selected for inclusion in the index, subject to periodic reviews.

As of November 5, 2025, HDV managed roughly $11.3 billion in total assets, invested across 76 holdings with an average market capitalization of $168.2 billion. The fund’s allocation skews heavily toward large-cap companies, which account for 79.2% of total assets, while mid-caps represent 18.5% of the fund. For comparison, the Russell 1000, a benchmark for large- and mid-cap stocks and represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), has a far larger average market cap of $354.7 billion, reflecting its substantial 42.2% weighting in mega-caps, while large caps still comprise 32.9% of the index, driven by its market cap-weighted structure.

HDV’s top ten holdings include Exxon Mobil (8.9%), Johnson & Johnson (6.7%), Chevron (6.6%), AbbVie (6.1%), Procter & Gamble (4.7%), Home Depot (4.2%), Coca-Cola (4.1%), Merck (4.1%), PepsiCo (4.0%), and Philip Morris International (3.9%). Together, they represent more than 50% of the fund, with a strong tilt toward mature and high dividend yield sectors like energy, healthcare, and consumer staples.

The table below lists a selection of U.S. dividend ETFs that serve as the peer group for HDV in this analysis. The Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) is the largest of the group, managing over $80 billion in assets, and tracking the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index, a broad and market-cap-weighted index benchmark. The SPDR S&P Dividend ETF (SDY) takes a more selective style, following the S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index, which includes only companies that have raised their dividends for at least 20 consecutive years, weighted by dividend yields. The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), on the other hand, blends quality and income, based on the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index, which screens for strong free cash flow, return on equity, and dividend growth, employing a modified market-cap methodology. Meanwhile, the iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY) emphasizes yield consistency, giving heavier weight to mid-cap names with historically stable payouts. The First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF (FDL) focuses on sustainability, tracking an index that selects the top 100 U.S. companies with stable and above-average dividends, while the ALPS Sector Dividend Dogs ETF (SDOG) applies an equal-weight approach, selecting the five highest-yielding stocks in each sector.

As HDV’s top ten holdings suggest, the fund shows a heavy tilt toward consumer staples (24.6%), energy (22.2%), and healthcare (21.0%), which together make up more than two-thirds of the portfolio. These defensive positions are followed by utilities (9.4%), technology (6.4%), and consumer discretionary (6.2%), while smaller allocations fall to communication services (3.5%), financial services (3.3%), industrials (2.4%), and basic materials (1.0%). Due to this sector mix, HDV is of course overweight in consumer staples (+20.0%), energy (+19.3%), healthcare (+11.7%), and utilities (+7.0%) relative to the Russell 1000, which is offset by a heavy underweight exposure to technology (-27.9%), financial services (-9.8%), communication services (-6.7%), and industrials (-6.0%).

Breaking down the fund’s portfolio across its largest sectors, we see that in consumer staples, HDV has 16 holdings in a somewhat concentrated allocation. Soft drinks is the largest segment, with global players Coca-Cola and PepsiCo. The allocation to the tobacco industry is anchored by Philip Morris and Altria Group. Household products are also significant, with exposure spread across Procter & Gamble, alongside Colgate-Palmolive, Kimberly-Clark, Kenvue, and Clorox. Smaller stakes include packaged foods & meats (e.g., Mondelez) and food & staples retailing through Sysco.

The energy allocation is heavily concentrated in two majors: Exxon Mobil and Chevron. Additional positions include ConocoPhillips, a pure-play upstream, while midstream exposure comes via Williams and Kinder Morgan. The fund also has a small stake in the oil-field services provider Schlumberger.

Within healthcare, the fund tilts heavily toward pharmaceuticals, with large positions in Johnson & Johnson, AbbVie, and Merck. There is also a single biotechnology name, Gilead Sciences, complemented by a stake in medical device maker Medtronic.

Outside the top three sectors, HDV has a few key positions in names such as Home Depot, Cisco Systems, and AT&T, all with more than 3% weight in the fund. The exposure to utilities is anchored by Duke Energy and Southern, while Blackstone and T. Rowe Price are the largest holdings in financial services, a surprisingly small sector in the fund, despite its above-average yields.

Given HDV’s focused allocation, the fund appears overweight in consumer staples (+11.5%), energy (+9.6%), and healthcare (+9.2%) relative to the peer group. These allocation gaps are amplified by peers’ allocation profiles that have been far from uniform. For example, DVY leans heavily into financial services (26.1%), SDY overweights industrials (18.4%), while FDL and SCHD favor energy (26.9% and 19.6%). As a result, HDV has weak sector correlation with most peers. The exceptions are SCHD and FDL, where sector correlation remains high at about 0.80. To be sure, HDV’s modest 3.3% allocation in financial services, a sector where dividend yields are typically high, only exacerbates these sector allocation gaps, highlighting different investment strategies adopted across dividend funds.

High Dividends, But Below The Peer Group Average

HDV’s 3.2% dividend yield is admittedly high compared to the Russell 1000’s 1.0% and market cap-weighted funds like VYM (2.5%) and SDY (2.7%). However, HDV still lags peers like SCHD (3.9%), FDL (4.0%), and SDOG (3.8%), as these also have relatively concentrated portfolios with a strong focus on yield. Meanwhile, dividend growth remains modest across the group, including HDV, with its low single-digit growth over the past three- and five-year periods.

Attractive Valuation, But Trading At A Premium Relative To Most Peers

At 16.2x P/E, HDV trades at a roughly 27% discount to the Russell 1000’s 22.1x. This is a broad-based discount, with valuation gaps exceeding 30% in consumer discretionary, technology, and communication services. The fund’s sector mix is also central to its valuation profile. Overweights in consumer staples, energy, and healthcare, all trading at 15-20x P/E, drive aggregate multiples lower, while minimal exposure to technology and industrials limits the impact of high-valuation segments that drive the Russell 1000’s premium. At the individual stock level, low P/E multiples in core sectors such as Exxon Mobil (16.4x), Chevron (19.8x), Merck (9.5x), AbbVie (17.6x), Altria (12.2x), and AT&T (14.3x) reinforce the fund’s value bias.

Given HDV’s focus on financial stability and yields, growth is modest. Its historical earnings growth of 3.1% is more than four percentage points below the Russell 1000’s 7.5%, while sales growth (2.7%) also trails the benchmark’s 7.4%. This modest pace stems from its sector allocation, as sectors with large allocations like consumer staples, healthcare, and energy are all typically slow-growth segments. Modest exposure to growth areas like technology and consumer discretionary does not help either.

Profitability falls short of the broader market as well. HDV’s EBITDA margin (33.4%) sits 2.2 percentage points below the Russell 1000 (35.6%), while ROE (37.8%) is well below the index’s 59.2%. The margin gap is concentrated in technology and consumer discretionary, due to the fund’s holdings, with no positions in high margin names, especially big tech such as NVIDIA and Microsoft. Meanwhile, core holdings such as Exxon Mobil, Chevron, PepsiCo, Cisco Systems, and Procter & Gamble all exhibit below-average margins that end up dragging the fund’s average profitability down. In short, despite holdings’ stable earnings in general, they cannot match the margin levels often seen in mega caps that have an oversized representation in broader equity indexes.

HDV trades at the higher end of the peer group’s valuation range, along with VYM and SDY, two market cap-weighted funds. With VYM allocating over 65% in large and mega, its above-average valuation makes sense relative to peers. On the other hand, lower valuations in DVY, FDL, and SDOG, all below 12x P/E, reflect an allocation profile much more skewed toward mid-caps when compared to other peers. In sum, while the sector allocation is not a key factor in valuation for these dividend funds, as core positions are largely allocated to defensive and value sectors, different market size allocations seem to be a much stronger factor influencing valuation as a whole. HDV’s heavy exposure to large caps just reinforces this point.

Lagging The Broader Market And Most Peers

HDV’s annualized returns of 8.5% (3-year) and 10.9% (5-year) have lagged behind the Russell 1000, which delivered 21.3% and 15.0%, respectively. The gap has been particularly wide in years of strong gains, such as 2023, when HDV underperformed its benchmark by more than ten percentage points. However, it held up much better in 2022, climbing 7.1% while the Russell 1000 declined 19.2%, underscoring the defensive nature of its portfolio.

Still, HDV’s returns look somewhat mixed relative to its peer group. The fund has lagged peers such as VYM, DVY, and FDL, though its steady profile helped it to outperform in the bear market of 2022, when the peer group’s average was 0.7%. The fund’s beta of 0.51 (3-year) and 0.64 (5-year) confirms its low-volatility profile, well below the broader market and also the peer group’s range of around 0.75–0.85.

Capture ratios reinforce this profile, with an upside capture of 51 (3-year) and 69 (5-year), as well as a downside capture of 70 (3-year) and 64 (5-year) that indicate a historical trend of participating in about two-thirds of market price action, lagging in rallies but limiting drawdowns during corrections. This helps explain its underperformance over the past years, amid an overall bullish environment.

With an average daily volume of $56.9 million, HDV’s liquidity is solid, though below the largest funds such as VYM and SCHD, which trade hundreds of millions per day. This results in a bid-ask spread of 0.02%, while all peers also exhibit narrow spreads. The fund’s turnover of 82% is elevated, pointing to more frequent trading activity relative to peers. On the other hand, its expense ratio of 0.08% sits well below the peer group average and is one of HDV’s structural advantages. The tracking error at around 10-11% is relatively high, but similar to other dividend funds. Finally, HDV’s Sharpe ratios of 0.38 (3-year) and 0.70 (5-year) are well below VYM, though broadly in line with peers like DVY and FDL. This illustrates that HDV’s lower volatility has at least partially offset weaker performance and helped drive risk-adjusted returns higher.

Final Thoughts

HDV’s portfolio reflects a strong quality bias. As such, its sector mix favors mature segments like consumer staples, healthcare, energy, and utilities, with a large-cap tilt. As a result, valuation is attractive compared to the Russell 1000, but it is a bit richer than other dividend funds with greater exposure to small caps and financial services. With a defensive, low-beta profile and a dividend yield of over 3%, the fund fits the demand for income and defensiveness amid a still resilient economy, along with policy uncertainty and elevated valuations that appear to support a balanced positioning.

Against this backdrop, the opportunity is clear in HDV: lower downside risk and reasonable yield. The flip side is the likelihood of continued relative underperformance in risk-on environments and a high exposure to oil price fluctuations, given the fund’s 22% allocation to the energy sector. In addition, it's very limited allocation to financial services reduces diversification and may be a drag on the fund when the economy recovers going forward, as it is a typical pro-growth sector. Thus, while HDV could serve as a defensive play, it is not the best option for investors seeking not only income, but total returns as well.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: 2 Small Deals List, As More Deals Join The Pipeline
data.description:
Two small IPOs and three SPACs debuted this week, as the US government shutdown finally came to an end. Five IPOs and four SPACs submitted initial filings.

Yacht retailer Off The Hook YS (OTH) priced its downsized IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $15 million at a $95 million market cap. The company claims to be the largest marine wholesaler in the industry, with eight physical locations and an online presence. Off The Hook also offers financing solutions for clients, as well as complementary vessel services and repairs. It finished the week down 8%.

Singapore-based microscopy tech provider Phaos Technology (POAS) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $14 million at a $114 million market cap. The company is focused on the assembly and commercialization of advanced microscopy-related solutions, technologies, and products for manufacturing, biomedical, and semiconductors, among other industries. With its patented microsphere-assisted technology, the company states that it provides the only commercially available advanced optical microscope that can see below the 200nm optical limit within a commercially viable working distance. Phaos Technology finished the week flat.

Three SPACs also priced this week. Alussa Energy Acquisition II (ALUB.U) raised $250 million to target energy and power infrastructure. Evolution Global Acquisition (EVOXU) raised $210 million in an upsized listing to target critical minerals. Blueport Acquisition (BPACU), led by a co-founder of law firm Ortoli Rosenstadt LLP, raised $50 million.

Week Ahead

Now that the longest government shutdown in history has concluded, the SEC is poised to resume regular operations. Three deals are on the calendar for the week ahead.

OTC-listed bank Central Bancompany (CBC) plans to raise $400 million at a $5.4 billion market cap. Central is a super-community bank with 156 full-service branches across 17 MSAs, with business predominantly located in Missouri, though it is growing its presence in the states of Kansas, Oklahoma, Colorado, and Florida. The bank’s cost of deposits is relatively low, allowing it to generate a strong net interest margin, though asset growth has been minimal, up just 1% in 2024.

Faith-focused technology platform Gloo Holdings (GLOO) plans to raise $100 million at an $801 million market cap. The company provides a vertical platform to faith-based organizations, with an emphasis on churches and Christian organizations, serving approximately 57,000 paying customers as of July 31, 2025. Gloo operates in a unique market, and while it has demonstrated strong growth, it has relied heavily on acquisitions as a driver.

Israeli regenerative medicine company Regentis Biomaterials (RGNT) is tentatively scheduled to raise $11 million at a $62 million market cap. Its lead candidate is GelrinC, a cell-free, off-the-shelf hydrogel implant for painful injuries to articular knee cartilage. GelrinC was approved as a device in Europe in 2017, and the company is currently carrying out a pivotal trial for pre-marketing approval in the US.

data.title: Invesco Growth And Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities rallied in the third quarter of 2025, driven by enthusiasm about artificial intelligence (AI), a resilient economy and anticipation of easier monetary policy from the US Federal Reserve (Fed).
  • Corporate earnings generally surpassed expectations, although forward guidance was cautious, particularly among companies dependent on consumers.
  • Fed policy appeared to heavily influence market sentiment as the combination of softer employment data and persistent inflation complicated its decision-making. Nevertheless, the Fed cut the federal funds rate by 0.25% at its September meeting, with markets reacting favorably.
  • The S&P 500 Index reached record highs during the quarter, ending with a return of 8.12%. Information technology (IT) and communication services stocks – especially those linked to AI – continued to outperform, while the more defensive consumer staples sector lagged.
  • Looking ahead, geopolitical tensions, elevated tariff rates and a US government shutdown may begin to weigh on the economy and prompt additional market volatility.
  • Regardless of the economic environment, we continue to focus on our fundamental analysis so we can move quickly to take advantage of new opportunities as they become available.
  • As always, we seek to invest in companies with attractive valuations and strong fundamentals, qualities we believe will ultimately be reflected in those companies’ stock prices.

New holdings:

Sempra: The utility company sold off following disappointing earnings guidance and concerns about its credit profile. However, Sempra has been shifting its strategic focus from its highly regulated California operations to its growing presence in Texas, which we believe offers greater potential for long-term growth.

Capital One Financial: The bank’s stock had declined after the announcement of its plan to acquire Discover Financial, which was completed in May. We believe the combined company should benefit from Capital One’s robust technology platform, increased scale, and synergies created by integrating Discover’s network.

Iqvia: Iqvia is one of the largest contract research organizations. Shares have come under pressure due to slower post-pandemic growth, tariffs and ongoing uncertainty surrounding drug pricing. However, we believe greater clarity on these macro issues, continued technology enhancements and share buybacks could help drive an earnings rebound.

Regeneron: Disappointing clinical trial results for several therapies, falling sales of its flagship macular degeneration treatment, and weak sentiment appear to have weighed on the drugmaker’s stock. Despite these challenges, Regeneron in our view has a strong balance sheet and a robust product pipeline. The company has also announced its first ever dividend.

Thermo Fisher Scientific: Despite near-term challenges that include trade tensions, National Institutes of Health (NIH) funding cuts and China uncertainty, we believe the company’s valuation is compelling. Its history of smart acquisitions and profit margin growth could drive the stock price higher as demand recovers after 2025.

Air Products & Chemicals: Burdened by costly projects and weak cash flow, Air Products has been undergoing a leadership change driven by activist shareholder pressure. We expect new management to focus on cost discipline and profit margin improvement, which we believe should benefit the stock.

Eliminated Holdings:

Centene: The stock dropped after the company pulled its full year 2025 earnings guidance due to weak growth in the health care exchange market, higher expected morbidity of participants, and rising medical costs in its Medicaid business. We sold this position in July.

GE Healthcare: The company has been facing headwinds from regulatory issues, tariff uncertainty and weakness in China. We sold the stock to fund another opportunity within the healthcare sector that we believe has a better risk/reward profile.

Fidelity National Information Services: The financial technology solutions provider reported earnings that were in line with estimates, but the stock price fell due to weaker-than-expected guidance.

Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earnings per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. data.title: Tracking Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Berkshire Hathaway’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Warren Buffett’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking 10 Years Of Berkshire Hathaway's Investment Portfolio article series for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the moves during Q2 2025.

During Q3 2025, Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) 13F stock portfolio value increased from ~$258B to ~$267B. The top five positions account for ~69% of the portfolio: Apple Inc., American Express, Bank of America, Coca Cola, and Chevron. There are 37 individual stock positions, many of which are small compared to the overall size of the portfolio.

Warren Buffett’s writings (pdfs) are a treasure trove of information and are a very good source for anyone starting out on individual investing. So are the classics The Intelligent Investor and Security Analysis.

Note 1: It was disclosed in August 2020 that Berkshire had built ~5% stakes in five Japanese trading businesses: ITOCHU, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo. The stakes increased to over 10% each as of Q1 2025. The 2021 AR had the following regarding the initial purchases in three of them: 5.6% ownership stake in ITOCHU Corporation (OTCPK:ITOCF) at a cost-basis of $23.52, 5.5% ownership stake in Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MSBHF) at a cost-basis of $8.57, and 5.7% ownership stake in Mitsui & Company (OTCPK:MITSF) at a cost-basis of $8.65. ITOCF, MSBHF, and MITSF currently trade at ~$60, ~$24.50, and ~$26.50 respectively. Mitsubishi had a 3-for-1 stock split in December 2023 and Mitsui had a 2-for-1 stock split in June 2024. The cost-basis are adjusted to reflect this.

Note 2: Berkshire Hathaway fully exited their stake in BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDY) by September 2025. Their ownership stake was ~20% as of Q3 2022. The stake fell below the 5% ownership threshold during Q2 2024.

New Stakes:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is a 1.62% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between ~$174 and ~$257. The stock currently trades above that range at ~$277.

Stake Disposals:

Atlanta Braves (BATRK) and D R Horton (DHI): These two positions were dropped during the quarter.

Stake Increases:

Chubb Limited (CB): CB is a 3.31% of the portfolio position purchased during H2 2023 at prices between ~$188 and ~$230. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$225 and ~$259. That was followed by a ~16% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$264 and ~$291. The stock currently trades at ~$296.

Note: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2023 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. They have a ~7.8% ownership stake in the business.

Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI): The stake came about as a result of a redistribution transaction by Liberty Media Group. The position was increased by ~315% during Q4 2023 as SIRI traded between ~$41 and ~$57. Q2 2024 saw another ~260% stake increase at prices between ~$25 and ~$38. That was followed by a ~12% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between $20.37 and $28.44. It currently trades at $21.51. This quarter also saw a ~4% stake increase.

Note: in December 2023, Liberty Media announced a plan to simplify the structure - Liberty SiriusXM to combine with Sirius XM to form a new entity. The consolidation ratios were 0.8375 of new SIRI for each LSXMK/LSXMA and 0.1 of new SIRI for each SIRI held. The transaction closed during Q1 2025. The prices quoted above are adjusted based on the conversion ratio.

Domino’s Pizza (DPZ): DPZ is a 0.48% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$402 and ~$504. There was a ~87% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$407 and ~$475. Q1 2025 saw a ~10% further increase at prices between ~$397 and ~$501. That was followed by a ~13% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$421 and ~$496. The stock currently trades at ~$410.

Note: they have a ~9% ownership stake in the business.

Lamar Advertising (LAMR): The very small 0.06% LAMR stake was established during the last quarter at prices between ~$100 and ~$123 and it is now above that range at ~$130. There was a minor ~3% stake increase this quarter.

Lennar Corp (LEN.B): The 0.34% Lennar stake saw a marginal increase this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Apple Inc. (AAPL): AAPL is currently the largest 13F portfolio stake by far at ~23%. It was built between Q1 2016 and Q1 2018 at a cost-basis of ~$35 per share. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$169 and ~$195. The next quarter saw another ~49% selling at prices between ~$165 and ~$216. That was followed by a ~25% reduction during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$207 and ~$234. The last quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$169 and ~$225. That was followed by a ~15% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$202 and ~$258. The stock currently trades at ~$272.

Bank of America (BAC): Berkshire established this large (top three) ~10% of the portfolio position through the exercise of Bank of America warrants. The warrants had a strike price of $7.14. The cost to exercise was $5B and it was funded using the $5B in 6% preferred stock they held. There was a ~30% stake increase in Q3 2018 at prices between $27.75 and $31.80. Q3 2024 saw a ~23% selling at prices between $36.41 and $43.84. That was followed by a ~15% reduction during the next quarter at prices between $39 and $47.51. Q1 2025 also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$39 and ~$48. That was followed by a ~4% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$33 and ~$48. This quarter also saw a ~6% trimming at prices between ~$45 and ~$53. The stock currently trades at $52.61.

Note: Berkshire’s overall cost-basis is ~$14 and ownership stake is at ~7.5%.

DaVita Inc. (DVA): DVA is a 1.60% of the portfolio position that was aggressively built over several quarters in the 2012-13 timeframe at prices between $30 and $49. The stock currently trades at ~$122 compared to Berkshire’s overall cost-basis of ~$45 per share. Q3 2020 saw a ~5% (2M shares) selling at $88 per share. The last three quarters also saw a ~14% trimming.

Note: Berkshire’s ownership stake in DaVita is ~40%.

Verisign Inc. (VRSN): VRSN was first purchased in Q4 2012 at prices between $34 and $49.50. The position was more than doubled in Q1 2013 at prices between $38 and $48. The buying continued till Q2 2014 at prices up to $63. This quarter saw a one-third selling at prices between ~$261 and ~$311. The stock currently trades at ~$253 and the position is at 0.94% of the portfolio (~10% of the business).

Nucor Corp (NUE): The 0.32% NUE stake saw a minor ~3% trimming this quarter.

Kept Steady:

American Express (AXP) and Coca Cola (KO): These two very large stakes were kept steady during the last ~10 years. Buffett has said these positions will be held permanently. Berkshire’s cost-basis on AXP and KO are at around $8.49 and $3.25 respectively and the ownership stakes are at ~20% and ~9.2% respectively.

Chevron Corp (CVX): CVX is a large (top five) ~7% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2020 at prices between ~$72 and ~$91. Q1 2021 saw a ~50% selling at prices between ~$85 and ~$112 while in Q3 2021 there was a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$94 and ~$106. That was followed with another one-third increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$102 and ~$119. Q1 2022 saw a whopping ~315% stake increase at prices between ~$119 and ~$171. The three quarters through Q3 2023 saw a one third selling at prices between ~$150 and ~$188. There was a ~14% stake increase during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$142 and ~$169. The stock currently trades at ~$158. H1 2024 saw a ~6% trimming while the last quarter saw a ~3% increase.

Note 1: Berkshire has a ~5.7% ownership stake in the business.

Note 2: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2020 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. An amendment filed later disclosed the activity.

Occidental Petroleum (OXY): Berkshire made a $10B investment in OXY in April 2019 through 100,000 preferred shares that has a liquidation value of $100,000 per share. Those shares pay 8% dividend, and the transaction came with warrants to purchase 83.86M shares at $59.62. In Q1 2022, Berkshire purchased ~136.4M shares at prices between ~$31 and ~$59 per share. That was followed with a ~22M share net-increase in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$60. The next quarter also saw a ~23% stake increase at prices between ~$57 and ~$75. The three quarters through Q2 2024 also saw a ~15% stake increase. The stock currently trades at $42.76. There was a minor ~3% stake increase during Q4 2024 and a marginal increase during the next quarter.

Note 1: Including warrants, Berkshire owns ~33% of the business (~349M shares). Those warrants expire one year after the preferred shares are fully redeemed. The tenth-year anniversary of preferred issuance from when they can be redeemed at 105% of PAR starts in 2029. 15% of the $10B in preferred was redeemed in 2023 at 110% of PAR (terms of the preferred required OXY to redeem a portion if they returned more capital to shareholders than the threshold specified).

Note 2: The publicly traded warrants under the symbol OXY.WS were initially distributed in June 2020 and allow holders to purchase one share of OXY common stock at $22 per share until August 3, 2027. A temporary offer allowed holders to exercise the warrants at a reduced price of $21.30 until March 31, 2025.

Moodys Inc. (MCO): MCO is a 4.40% of the 13F portfolio stake. It is a very long-term position and Buffett’s cost-basis is $10.05. The stock currently trades at ~$480. Berkshire controls ~13% of the business.

Kraft Heinz Co. (KHC): KHC is currently a fairly large position at 3.17% of the portfolio. Kraft Heinz started trading in July 2015 with Berkshire owning just over 325M shares (~26.5% of the business). The stake came about because of two transactions with 3G capital as partner: a ~$4B net investment in 2013 for half of Heinz and a ~$5B investment for the acquisition of Kraft Foods Group in early 2015. Berkshire’s cost-basis on KHC is ~$30 per share compared to the current price of $24.85.

Kroger Company (KR): KR is a 1.26% of the portfolio position established in Q4 2019 at prices between $24 and $29. The five quarters through Q3 2021 had seen a ~225% stake increase at prices between ~$30 and ~$40. The three quarters through Q3 2022 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$44 and ~$52. The stock currently trades at $66.71.

Note: Berkshire’s ownership stake in Kroger is ~7%.

Amazon (AMZN): AMZN is a 0.82% of the portfolio stake established during Q1 2019 at prices between ~$75 and ~$91. The stake was increased by ~11% in the next quarter at prices between ~$85 and ~$98. The stock currently trades at ~$235. Q3 2023 saw a ~5% trimming.

Liberty Media Corp (FWONK) (LLYVK) (LLYVA): These tracking stocks were first acquired as a result of Liberty Media’s recapitalization in April 2016. Shareholders received 1 share of Liberty SiriusXM Group, 0.25 shares of Liberty Media Group and 0.1 shares of Liberty Braves Group for each share held. Berkshire held 30M shares of Liberty Media for which they received the same amount of Liberty SiriusXM Group shares. Since then, there have been several transactions, redistributions, etc. Last August saw another reclassification whereby Liberty SiriusXM common stock was reclassified, and shareholders received 0.25 shares of Liberty Live for each share of Liberty SiriusXM. Also, Liberty Formula One was reclassified, and shareholders received 0.0428 shares of Liberty Live for each share of Liberty Formula One. The net share count increased by ~23% as a result of these transactions. The Liberty SiriusXM stake saw a ~55% stake increase during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$28.50 and ~$31.25. FWONK and LLYVK currently trade at ~$99 and ~$84 respectively. There was a ~18% overall reduction during the last two quarters.

Constellation Brands (STZ): STZ is a 0.68% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$218 and ~$255. The next quarter saw the position increased by ~114% at prices between ~$161 and ~$229. That was followed by a ~12% increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$159 and ~$197. The stock currently trades at ~$129.

Note: they have a ~7.4% ownership stake in the business.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH): UNH is a 0.65% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$249 and ~$606. The stock currently trades at ~$322.

Capital One Financial (COF): The 0.57% of the portfolio stake in COF was purchased during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$90 and ~$122. There was a ~25% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$86 and ~$114. Q2 2024 saw a ~21% reduction at prices between ~$133 and ~$148. The next quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$131 and ~$153. That was followed by a ~18% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$145 and ~$192. The stock currently trades at ~$211.

Aon plc (AON): AON is a 0.55% of the portfolio position established during Q1 2021 at prices between ~$202 and ~$234. The next quarter saw a ~7% increase at prices between ~$230 and ~$259. The stock currently trades at ~$350.

Ally Financial (ALLY): The 0.43% ALLY stake saw a whopping ~235% increase during Q2 2022 at prices between ~$32 and ~$53. The stock currently trades at $39.29.

Note: Berkshire has a ~9.6% ownership stake in Ally Financial.

Pool Corp (POOL): The 0.40% of the portfolio POOL stake was established during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$294 and ~$372. The next quarter saw a ~48% stake increase at prices between ~$339 and ~$390. That was followed by a ~145% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$312 and ~$375. The last quarter saw another ~135% stake increase at prices between ~$282 and ~$327. The stock is now below their purchase price ranges at ~$242.

Note: They have 9.2% ownership stake in the business.

Louisiana-Pacific (LPX): The 0.19% LPX position was established during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$48.40 and ~$65. There was a ~22% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$51 and ~$66. The stock is now at $75.41. There was a ~6% trimming during Q1 2024 and that was followed by a ~10% trimming during the next quarter. Q4 2024 also saw a ~5% trimming.

Note: Berkshire has a ~8% ownership stake in the business.

Heico Holdings (HEI): The 0.12% HEI stake was purchased during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$188 and ~$229. There was a ~11% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$217 and ~$273. That was followed by a similar increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$229 and ~$328. The stock is now at ~$314.

Charter Communications (CHTR): CHTR is a 0.11% of the portfolio position. It was established during the last three quarters of 2014 at prices between $118 and $170. In Q2 2015, the position was increased by ~42% at prices between $168 and $193 and that was followed with another ~21% increase the following quarter at prices between $167 and $195. The six quarters thru Q4 2018 had seen a combined ~25% selling at prices between $250 and $395 and that was followed with a ~20% reduction in Q1 2019 at prices between $285 and $366. H2 2021 saw another ~25% reduction at prices between ~$605 and ~$821. That was followed by similar selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$291 and ~$383. data.title: Peloton: The Turnaround Has Stalled Without Growth
data.description:
Peloton Interactive, Inc. (PTON) is a company that has been on a ride. I have followed the stock and company for years. The stock came onto the scene during COVID and went crazy. The company made its IPO on September 26, 2019 at $29 a share. The stock hit the market at just the right time with the COVID lockdowns and everyone working out at home. The stock jumped above $150 a share by the end of 2020. It was the up and coming growth stock….. Until it wasn’t. Just look at the chart since the company went public.

Demand for Peloton bikes exploded during COVID. The company operated as if that demand would continue. The company made a lot of missteps during their growth period. They overhired, they paid too much for hardware as they tried to get it to market quickly, they tried to get into manufacturing, made some bad acquisitions, and mismanaged cash flow amidst growth. That demand and growth did not continue once gyms opened back up. Business slowed down and the company was overstaffed, had supply chain issues, and was burning cash. I covered what I thought were management missteps more in depth when I covered the stock a few years ago.

Over the past few years the stock has been pretty range bound. There has been some small fluctuations up and down but overall there has not been large movements in the stock. Over the past year the stock is down nearly 12%.

A lot of what I thought a few years ago seems to be playing out. The company has a good product. It has a loyal customer base. It just needed to clean up operations to get the company to a profitable position. It has taken some time for that to play out, longer than I thought it would take. Over the past few years the company has cleaned up its supply chain and operations and improved margins, it has leaned more and more into high margin subscriptions, and has right sized the business to better match with revenues. The company has cleaned up the operations to the point where they were profitable the last 2 quarters. Now that the company seems to have the operations in order it needs to return to revenue growth to see the stock price move upwards.

Financial Review

Peloton reported revenue of $551 million during Q1 2026, that is a decrease of 6% from the prior year. Revenue declines continue to be a concern for the company. The biggest achievement for the last two quarters is that the company reached profitability. The margin profile has continued to improve over the past few years. Gross margin did see a slight decrease from the prior year due to a $13.5 million charge due to an issue with their bike seat. Even with that recall the company had a gross margin of 51.5%, which was only down by 30 basis points. Without that charge the company would have had a margin of 54%. The company saw decline in revenues year over year but were able to turn a profit due to improving margins and then also reducing operating expenses. The company cut operating expenses from $291.2 million to $242.4 million. Net income came in at $14 million and adjusted EBITDA was $118 million for the quarter both increases from the prior year.

The company has finally pushed itself to a profitable position. This is a great job by the company but also I figured it would have happened much quicker. The company has been pretty slow on its restructure. Two and a half years ago when I covered the stock I noted that the company has a long road to go to become profitable, I did not think that long road was quite this long.

One of the big items of concern previously for the company was that it was losing money on each piece of hardware that it sold. It was making money on the subscriptions. This was not sustainable. The hardware business will always have a lower margin but the subscription business could not make up for the loss being attributed to each sale. The company has had a positive gross margin on the “Connected Fitness” business for over a year now. Without the one time charge in Q1 the margin would have been 15.8% for the Connected Fitness Products. This was part of the process of working with third parties on the manufacturing. It is good to see that has been addressed.

The biggest concern on the financials is the continued decline in subscribers. The company has been seeing a steady drop in the number of subscribers. This is the cash cow for the company, but that's also partly to be expected. As sales have slowed the subscriptions would slow as well. The company really needs to stop the decline. It does not appear that the decline is done either, as the company projects a decline for the whole of 2026.

The other item that is concerning is the continued decline in revenues. The company has reduced expenses and the size of the company to be profitable but it needs to see revenue growth. It is hard for the stock price to have much movement if the company continues to shrink.

Outlook

The company provided estimates for next quarter and the 2026 year as a whole. Both outlooks provided a similar picture. Continued declines in revenues and subscribers, yet there is profitability. For Q2 the company expects revenues to be flat for the most part and for the year to be down 2% from the prior year. The decline is definitely getting smaller, and for Q2 is expected to be flat. Connected Fitness Subscriptions is expected to continue to fall. Gross margins are expected to be up by 100 basis points for the year overall. The company also plans to increase its profitability by continuing its cost reductions. This shows as it raised EBITDA to $425-$475 from $403 in 2025 despite revenues declining. One of the strengths the company is estimating is free cash flow. The company expects that free cash flow will be at least $250 million for the year.

Similar to the current report there are positives, the company is increasing its profitability, and negatives, continued declines in subscriptions and revenue. These declines do seem to be easing going forward. The company seems to have its ducks in order on the operations at the moment. It is cash flow positive. It is in a position to find growth again.

Catalysts

I wonder what will push the company back to growth. The company announced back in October that it is releasing a new line of commercial fitness equipment. It also announced that it will integrate AI software into its products as well as some new hardware for its products. It does not seem that either of these are expected to drive revenues much in 2026 as the company still expects a decrease in revenues year over year.

I think the commercial market is still the growth market for Peloton. It acquired Precor years ago and at a bad time for the company. I think it has finally put itself into a position to being pushing sales on the commercial side of things. Management also discussed international markets being a growth potential within the B2B space. It does not appear this is expected to pick up too much traction over the next 3 quarters but I think this has the best potential going forward.

Peloton has used its profitability to strengthen its balance sheet. The company ended the quarter with over $1 billion in cash and net debt of $395 million. The company decreased debt by $382 million. The company will be able to use the cash flow to deleverage the balance sheet. This could become a position of real strength for the company going forward if it can maintain its profitability. Management discussed during the call using some of this capital for buying back stock or even potentially going to cash dividends. The cash strength could help push the stock higher as well.

One outlier catalyst that I don’t think is outside the question would be an acquisition of the company. I floated this a few years ago and there were some rumors for a while about an acquisition. Now that the company is profitable and also has strong cash flows it could be a target. I think the company could be enticing to private equity. Taking it private would cut out a lot of costs as well.

Valuation

The stock trades at a P/E of 57.46x for the expected results of 2026. That is not a cheap stock. I will say that progress is being made on the profitability front. The only problem is that with a P/E that high I would expect to see growth on the revenue as well as earnings front. Without any revenue growth this is a hard P/E to justify.

The quant rating is a BUY.

A big part of this is due to the “growth” factor being an A+. That rating is due to the company becoming profitable. Since the company has been loss making in prior years, EBITDA and Operating Cash Flow growth are through the roof on a percentage basis. I don’t think this tells the whole story going forward. Without any revenue growth the EBITDA and Operating Cash Flow are going to have a hard time maintaining high growth rates. Once the company laps the quarters of profitability then that growth is going to be much smaller. The company can continue to cut costs where it can and push profits slightly higher, like they have forecast for 2026, but there are limitations on how much profit they can pull from the system without overall company growth. That A+ rating is likely to drop and so will the buy rating.

Risks

Peloton is still seeing a decline in its business overall. Revenues continue to decline and subscribers continue to fall. The company is at risk of continuing to become a smaller company, which does not bode well for the stock price.

Macro risks are a big factor for the company. They are selling a premium product. If the market slows down then I can see the want of a Peloton getting cut out of the budget pretty quickly for users. I do think there is some cushion here as higher end products seem to hold during slowdowns better, but I think sales could take a hit.

Tariffs are another concern. This seems to be waning as more trade deals are made and also debates about whether they will stand at all. The company already has the tariffs on their current products and future projections. If a trade dispute were to occur and tariffs increase it would affect the company’s profits.

Conclusion

Peloton has come a long way in the past few years. The company has right sized their operations. They improved their operations on the production, fulfillment, and distribution of their products. This has allowed them to improve margins. They have also cut operating costs significantly. This combination has made the company profitable.

Despite the company being profitable it continues to see revenues decline. The company also continues to lose subscribers. After improving its operations the company is in a position to look towards growth. The biggest potential driver of that would be the commercial market. It does not appear that growth is going to take hold during the next 3 quarters as the company has forecast a decline in revenues for the year. The company is also in a strong capital position and could return value to shareholders through its cash.

I think the stock is much more enticing than it was when I reviewed it a few years ago. It has made significant progress and become profitable. I am still going to rate the stock a hold. I want to see the company show signs of growth before I would look to invest. It does not seem Peloton is there yet.

data.title: International Annual Returns, The Breakout In The Nikkei, And Top 10 Holdings
data.description:
It’s no mystery that international has outperformed the S&P 500 in 2025, and not by a small amount.

The dollar’s direction will likely determine whether 2026 is a repeat of 2025 for the asset class, and the top strategists on Wall Street, like David Kelly of JPMorgan and Rick Rieder of BlackRock, have both said the dollar is still too strong and needs to come down more.

Japan and the Nikkei

Callum Thomas of TopDownCharts, who hails from New Zealand, has been posting on X and LinkedIn for years and does a good job.

This chart above from TopDown Charts shows the breakout in the Nikkei 225, the Nikkei’s first poke of its head above the 1989 high - and it happened in just the last year.

This blog is playing the breakout via the EWJ or the iShares MSCI Japan Index Fund ETF.

Here are the annual returns for EWJ as of 10/31/25:

  • 1-year: +25.52%
  • 3-year: +20.79%
  • 5-year: +9.35%
  • 10-year: +7.25%
  • 15-year: +6.77%

Morningstar’s returns track something called “earliest available” annual return, which for the EWJ would be its first trading month, which is December 1991, or two years after the Nikkei’s peak in 1989, and that “earliest available annual return” is just +2.85% over the last 33 years.

The 5-year return is still less than 10%, and note the spreadsheet at the top of this page, and how the 10 and 15-year returns are still below 10%. In the last 12 to 18 months, international as an asset class has closed the gap with the S&P 500, but the sentiment around non-US is far less frothy than the US.

Top 10–12 Holdings as of 10/31/25:

  • JP Morgan Income Fund (JMSIX): YTD return +6.33%
  • JPMorgan (JPM) common equity: YTD return +32.11%
  • JPMorgan Developed Int’l Value (JFEAX): YTD return +36.05%
  • Amazon common stock (AMZN): +11.32%
  • Microsoft (MSFT) common equity: +23.44%
  • Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) equity ETF: +23.51%
  • Schwab (SCHW) common equity: +28.81%
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) common equity: +48.87%
  • IBM common equity (IBM): +42.13%
  • Tesla common equity (TSLA): +13.05%
  • iShares Emerging Market ex-China (EMXC): +31.81%
  • iShares +20-year Treasuries (TLT): +6.80%

S&P 500 YTD return as of 10/31/25 was +17.40%

  • This blog manages $55 million via separate accounts at Charles Schwab and has done so since 1995. There are 4 to 5 larger accounts that represent roughly 20% of the $55 million, and several of those are 100% equity.
  • The three JPMorgan positions were not intentional, although JPMorgan has been very supportive of this blog’s work, and when this blog was looking for an international fund/ETF in late ’24 to reallocate to, the JFEAX fund – even in ’24 – was performing ahead of those funds followed.
  • Most client accounts are managed on a “balanced” basis with allocations targeted towards 60% equity/40% fixed income mix.
  • The large-cap tech positions have been trimmed over the last 12 to 14 months, since this secular bull market is now in its 15th year, with the start coming on March 9, 2009. Rather than rebalance at the end of a quarter, the rebalancing is done almost continually, with rotation from what has outperformed materially to an asset class, sector, or stock that has underperformed materially. It takes time to sell larger positions, often due to tax constraints, and it often takes time to build out-of-favor positions into material positions.
  • The three newer positions in 2025 were JFEAX, EMXC, and IBM. Not on the top 10 list yet, but shares are still being added are Boeing (BA), Japan Nikkei ETF (EWJ) and Nike (NKE).

Summary/conclusion

Having lived through the 2000s decade, when international investing completely missed the late 1990s bull market and only began to outperform in mid-2000 through 2007, only to collapse once again in 2008, 2025 was likely one of the best years of non-US asset class returns since the late 2000s.

In my opinion (and it’s only that), the “China-centric” gravitational pull from 2000 to 2007, when China was growing 15% per year, from 2000 to 2010, 2011 (and maybe longer), may be the reason the international outperformance lasted only several years in the 2000s. Everything non-US was dependent on that Chinese economic engine.

The fact that China is no longer as dominant as it once was may actually be a reason international returns could see a longer streak of high single-digit, low double-digit returns over the next few years, given the asset class has been dead money for so long.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of this information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: FOF: Monthly Income With Solid Total Return
data.description:
I rate Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund (NYSE:FOF) a Buy, for income-focused investors who are looking for long term buy and hold investments. The fund is focused on total return, generating monthly income while building capital appreciation. FOF is a fund of funds holding a highly diverse set of investments, including bonds from PIMCO, commodities such as gold and silver with Sprott, and various BlackRock Capital allocation funds.

The consistent monthly distribution is a reliable source of income for retirement or to use for dollar cost averaging. Given the current environment, FOF has the potential to act as a defensive income fund, weathering a potential correction. Although its trading at a slight premium to NAV (1.86%), the distribution of 7.96% makes the fund appealing in my view.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the fund are:

  • Pays a consistent monthly distribution of $0.087 since 2016.
  • Currently yields 7.96% annually, goes ex-dividend on 12/9/25.
  • Highly diversified fund composed of top tier closed-end funds.
  • Actively managed by Cohen & Steers, a highly reputable money manager.
  • Fund has been running for 19 years.
  • Low expense ratio of 0.95%.
  • Strong total return over 5 years (79.74%) and 10 years (183.4%).

Full disclosure, I own shares in FOF, and a few other Cohen & Steers closed end funds, which have performed well over time. In fact, I recently published an analysis on all of the C&S closed-end funds, recommending certain picks in the article: “Best High Yield Income Funds from Cohen & Steers.” Click on article to read.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the fund.

Cohen & Steers – Leading Global Investment Managers

Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS) is based in New York City and has been in business for over thirty-five years with $90.9 billion in assets under management (AUM). 74% of the AUM is focused on listed real assets, 19% on alternative income and the remaining 7% on multi-strategy funds. With six offices globally, C&S’s senior management have twenty years on average of investment experience. The company offers both private and public funds in the form of open and closed-end funds.

C&S serves a primarily institutional client. They offer collective investment trusts (CITs) for qualified defined benefit and defined contribution plans. C&S also provide customized investment strategies to institutional investors, such as large pension funds, endowments, and foundations.

Founded in 1986, Marty Cohen and Bob Steers launched the first public mutual fund specializing in real estate securities. In 1988, the company launched the first closed-end real estate securities fund. Per their Factsheet:

Cohen & Steers is a leading global investment manager specializing in real assets and alternative income, focused on delivering attractive returns, income and diversification.

Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund

According to the Fact Sheet:

“The investment objective of the Fund is to seek to achieve total return consisting of high current income and potential capital appreciation through investments in the common stock of closed-end management investment companies that invest significantly in equity or income-producing securities.”

The top 10 holdings account for 45.9% of the portfolio as listed below. Notice the sectors of each fund including commodities, equity, and fixed income. Also the top 7 funds are trading at a discount to NAV.

The fund is diversified, as seen in the chart below, into equity, commodity, fixed income, and municipal funds, making it ideal for buy and hold, in my view. The equity component which comprises 54% of the fund will generate capital appreciation for total return, and potentially capital gains for distribution.

Performance To Date

Over the past 10 years, an investment of $10,000 in FOF would have grown to $28,340 with dividends reinvested. If an investor took the distributions for income, the fund would still be worth $11,580. This means for most investors, the share price grew while they collected distributions. This feature is especially attractive for retired investors relying on a monthly income or institutional investors constructing defined benefit plans, such as pensions.

In comparison to benchmarks over the past 10 years, FOF produced a total return of 183.4%, outperforming the real estate sector represented by (VNQ) but falls short of the S&P 500 (SPY), as seen in the chart below.

Valuation

In 2016, the fund switched from quarterly to monthly distributions, paying a consistent $0.0870 per share every month.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, FOF has the lowest expense ratio, at just less than 1%. The turnover of funds at 30% is typical for the sector, but still shows the fund is actively managed. Beyond the 8.0% distribution yield, FOF generated a 14.0% 5-year price performance.

Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF) is the largest fund ranked by assets under management (AUM). The dividend yield is similar at 8.0% but the share price has declined -6.5%.

RiverNorth Opportunities (RIV) pays the highest distribution rate of 13.2% but that comes with a declining share price, -20.3% over the past 5 years. It also has a high turnover rate at 58%. Since the last time I wrote about this fund, RIV increased its AUM to pass CEFS.

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) is one of the most popular funds discussed on Seeking Alpha, run by activist investor Saba Management. Typically the fund pays a special distribution at the end of the year. It has the highest expense ratio of 4.3% but has generated strong returns indicated by its 8.3% yield and best in class 19.0% 5-year price performance. Many analysts on Seeking Alpha tend to prefer CEFS over FOF for the ability to outperform over the long term.

High Income Securities Fund (PCF) is similar to RIV in that the distribution rate is higher at 11.4% but has a declining share price -18.9% and the highest turnover rate of 81%.

Over the past 10 years, as seen in the chart below, FOF generated 125.4% total return, falling second only to CEFS with 151.6%. In essence, an investment in FOF more than doubled its money for the investor.

Outlook

As the stock market has hit multiple highs recently, a prudent investor would be wise to look for defensive investments in my view. FOF provides both conservative active management and diversification which is a strong combination to prevent capital loss during a correction. FOF is well positioned during a stock market drop to continue paying the same monthly income with the ability to dollar cost average, achieving a lower cost basis.

Risks To Rating

Within the short term, many analysts are calling for a stock market correction as a result of the recent run up, which could adversely affect the share price of the CEF.

Another risk to the fund of fund strategies is that an investor may be able to outperform the fund by holding a few of the funds by themselves and not paying the management fee. What I like about FOF is that the funds are well selected, and if they are underperforming, C&S will switch them out. Therefore the fee for active management is worth it, in my view, to prevent holding the wrong funds.

Bottom Line

I rate Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund a Buy based on its consistent monthly distribution rate currently yielding 8%. It’s focus is on total return, so it not only pays a consistent monthly distribution but generates capital appreciation along the way. The fund holds a highly diverse set of closed end funds including bonds from PIMCO, commodities such as gold and silver, and assorted BlackRock equity allocation funds.

FOF is a consistent performer vs. peers. Run by Cohen & Steers, a highly reputable money manager, the fund has been in existence for 19 years and has the lowest expense ratio vs. competition. When benchmarked to typical indexes, FOF outperforms the real estate sector but falls short of the S&P 500. FOF has a strong total return of 125.4% over 10 years.

Risks to the fund include a drop in the stock market after achieving a record high, causing the NAV to trade at a discount. Investors could also just buy the funds individually, not paying the management fee for a fund of fund strategy. Within the sector, I regard Saba’s CEFs as the closest competitor, which may outperform FOF over the long term given their activist approach and payment of special distributions at the end of the year.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Volatile Friday Ends With Weekly Gain
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 experienced a volatile Friday, which ultimately ended with the index posting a minor weekly gain of 0.1%. This marks the fourth weekly gain in the past five weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53.

This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new, then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%).

Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.96%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index that includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 14.75% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 7.12% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).
